cards.q: Reduce API cost: you have latency-sensitive pre-merge checks AND overnight tech-debt reports. How do you split them?
cards.a: Batch for the overnight reports only; keep real-time for pre-merge checks.
cards.why: Match each API to its latency need — the Batches API (up to 24hr) suits reports, not blocking checks devs are waiting on.

cards.q: Of your CI review modes, which is the best candidate to move to the Batches API?
cards.a: Deep analysis only.
cards.why: It already runs overnight, tolerates latency, and polls for completion — a perfect match for the async, poll-based batch model (50% savings).

cards.q: CI runs the Claude Code CLI in --print mode; you need reliable structured findings posted as inline PR comments.
cards.a: Use --output-format json + --json-schema, then parse and post via the GitHub API.
cards.why: Native CLI enforcement guarantees well-formed JSON with the required fields — more reliable than prompt-only formatting.

cards.q: Three tasks: PR style checks, weekly security audits, nightly test generation. Which go to the Batches API?
cards.a: Sync for PR style checks; Batches API for the weekly audits + nightly tests.
cards.why: PR checks block developers (need immediate sync); audits and nightly tests are scheduled and tolerate the 24hr window. Not all three on batch.

cards.q: A PR touches 14 files; a single review pass gives uneven attention. How do you get consistent depth + catch cross-file issues?
cards.a: Split into focused per-file passes for local issues + one separate integration pass for cross-file data flow.
cards.why: Addresses the root cause (attention dilution). Consensus voting across 3 full runs suppresses real bugs that only appear intermittently.

cards.q: ~15 findings/PR; devs spend too long investigating each. Stakeholders rejected filtering. What helps?
cards.a: Require Claude to include reasoning + confidence inline with each finding.
cards.why: Faster triage, nothing hidden. Auto-suppressing FP-signature findings is still filtering — which was explicitly rejected.

cards.q: Reviews find valid issues but the output format is inconsistent. Most reliable fix?
cards.a: Add 3-4 few-shot examples showing the exact format (issue, location, specific fix).
cards.why: Concrete format examples beat abstract instructions for consistency.

cards.q: ~50% FP rate is eroding dev trust NOW. Improving prompts will take weeks. What do you do in the meantime?
cards.a: Temporarily disable the high-FP categories (style, naming, docs); run only high-precision ones while you fix prompts.
cards.why: Stops trust erosion immediately. Keeping everything on for weeks lets devs keep dismissing all findings.

cards.q: Your pipeline runs claude "Analyze..." and the job hangs waiting for input. Fix?
cards.a: Add the -p (--print) flag.
cards.why: Runs non-interactively, outputs to stdout, exits. CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true is not a real feature.

cards.q: Your review works iteratively (analyze file → call tool → continue). Why can't the Batches API run this?
cards.a: The async fire-and-forget model can't execute a tool mid-request and return results for Claude to continue.
cards.why: It breaks iterative tool-calling. The limitation is architectural — not just the 24hr latency.

cards.q: Your review suggests test cases but duplicates existing coverage. Fix?
cards.a: Include the existing test file in context so Claude sees what's already covered.
cards.why: Gives Claude the information to avoid redundant suggestions.

cards.q: Severity ratings are inconsistent for similar issues. How do you stabilize them?
cards.a: Put explicit severity criteria in the prompt with concrete code examples for each level.
cards.why: Removes ambiguity about what each level means; gives clear reference points.

cards.q: Review flags comments/docstrings unreliably. The instruction is vague. Best fix that generalizes?
cards.a: Specify the explicit criterion: flag a comment only when its claimed behavior contradicts the actual code.
cards.why: Defining the criterion beats few-shot examples, which won't generalize to novel contradiction types.

cards.q: After a re-push, the review re-reports issues already fixed. Stop redundant comments?
cards.a: Include prior review findings in context; instruct Claude to report only new/still-unaddressed issues.
cards.why: A post-processing match-filter is brittle — LLM comment wording varies across runs.

cards.q: Claude generated code, considered the edge cases, concluded it was correct — but it's wrong. More thinking didn't help. What does?
cards.a: Have a second, independent Claude review the changes without seeing the generator's reasoning.
cards.why: Eliminates confirmation bias — a fresh perspective, like human peer review. More deliberation can't un-rationalize a decision already made.

cards.q: Research output missed whole domains (music, writing, film); the subagents all executed their tasks fine. Root cause?
cards.a: The coordinator's task decomposition was too narrow.
cards.why: It only assigned visual-arts subtasks. The decomposition — not the subagents — caused the missing coverage.

cards.q: Web search returns results for only 3 of 5 source categories. How should synthesis handle it?
cards.a: Structure the synthesis output with coverage annotations: which findings are well-supported vs which areas have gaps.
cards.why: Graceful degradation with transparency. Erroring out throws away all the work that did succeed.

cards.q: The doc-analysis agent has a general fetch_url tool and keeps doing web searches with it. Fix?
cards.a: Replace fetch_url with a scoped load_document tool that validates URLs point to document formats.
cards.why: Constrain capability at the interface (least privilege) so the wrong behavior is impossible — don't remove the legit ability.

cards.q: Web search + doc analysis both finished. What happens next in the orchestrator-workers pattern?
cards.a: The coordinator passes both sets of findings to the synthesis agent for unified integration.
cards.why: The coordinator maintains centralized control: collect from subagents, route to the next component.

cards.q: Doc-analysis subagent hits a corrupted PDF it can't parse. Best response?
cards.a: Return the error with context to the coordinator and let it decide.
cards.why: Keeps the failure visible; the coordinator has the context to skip, retry differently, or notify the user.

cards.q: Combined upstream output is 85K tokens of verbose content, blowing up synthesis cost. Fix at the source?
cards.a: Modify upstream agents to return structured data (key facts, citations, relevance scores) instead of verbose content.
cards.why: Cuts token volume where it's generated, preserving the essential info for synthesis.

cards.q: Synthesis agent constantly round-trips to web search for simple fact checks (85% are simple). Reduce latency how?
cards.a: Give synthesis a scoped verify_fact tool for simple lookups; keep delegating the 15% complex ones via the coordinator.
cards.why: Handles most lookups directly (least privilege) while preserving the delegation path for hard cases.

cards.q: Doc-analysis subagent frequently hits transient PDF failures. How should it handle errors?
cards.a: Implement local recovery for transient failures; only propagate the truly unresolvable ones — with what was attempted + partial results.
cards.why: Handle errors at the lowest capable level. Masking errors as success hides real data-quality problems.

cards.q: Why route all subagent communication through the coordinator (hub-and-spoke) vs agent-to-agent?
cards.a: The coordinator can observe all interactions, handle errors consistently, and control what info each subagent receives.
cards.why: These are the core advantages of centralized hub-and-spoke communication.

cards.q: Web search subagent times out on a complex topic. What should it return?
cards.a: Structured error context: failure type, attempted query, partial results, and potential alternative approaches.
cards.why: Gives the coordinator everything it needs to make an intelligent recovery decision.

cards.q: Coordinator mis-routes requests — the web-search and doc-analysis tools have semantically overlapping descriptions. Fix?
cards.a: Rename the web search tool (e.g. extract_web_results) and update its description so each tool's purpose is unambiguous.
cards.why: Fixes the root cause (semantic overlap). Few-shot routing examples just patch the symptom.

cards.q: Web search: one source times out, another returns "0 results." Treat them the same?
cards.a: No — distinguish access failures (timeout → retry) from valid empty results ("0 results" = a successful query).
cards.why: They're semantically distinct outcomes needing different responses. Retrying a definitively-empty source wastes resources.

cards.q: Doc-analysis agent finds two credible sources with directly conflicting figures. What should it do?
cards.a: Complete the analysis with both figures included, annotate the conflict with source attribution, and let the coordinator reconcile.
cards.why: Picking a winner oversteps its role — it lacks the broader context. Don't bury a real discrepancy in a footnote.

cards.q: Synthesis quality is inconsistent; key info gets lost in large aggregated input (lost-in-the-middle). Fix?
cards.a: Put a key-findings summary at the start (primacy) and add explicit section headers to aid navigation.
cards.why: Leverages the primacy effect and helps the model attend to middle content. Aggressive summarizing risks worse info loss.

cards.q: Different conventions for React components vs API routes vs tests, applied automatically by file path. What mechanism?
cards.a: Rule files in .claude/rules/ with YAML frontmatter + glob patterns (e.g. **/*.test.tsx).
cards.why: Globs apply conventions deterministically by path. Skills need manual invocation — wrong fit for automatic, path-based rules.

cards.q: You're restructuring a monolith into microservices across dozens of files. Best first move?
cards.a: Enter plan mode — explore the codebase, understand dependencies, design the approach before changing anything.
cards.why: Safe exploration + informed decisions about service boundaries before costly changes.

cards.q: You want a custom /review slash command, version-controlled for the whole team. Where does it go?
cards.a: In the .claude/commands/ directory in the project repo.
cards.why: Version-controlled and auto-available to everyone who clones. CLAUDE.md is for instructions/context, not command definitions.

cards.q: A guideline works for original devs but new hires don't get it. Likely cause + fix?
cards.a: It lives in the original devs' user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md — move it to the project's .claude/CLAUDE.md.
cards.why: Project-level config reaches all current and future team members automatically.

cards.q: Add a GitHub MCP server for the team, with auth, without committing secrets. How?
cards.a: Add it to a project-scoped .mcp.json using env var expansion (${GITHUB_TOKEN}); document the var in the README.
cards.why: One version-controlled source of truth; each dev supplies their own credential. A custom token-proxy wrapper is over-engineered.

cards.q: An /analyze-codebase skill produces huge output that pollutes the main context. Fix?
cards.a: Add context: fork to the skill's frontmatter — runs it in an isolated sub-agent context.
cards.why: Verbose output stays out of the main session. Splitting into 3 skills produces the same total output.

cards.q: A /migration skill needs: show expected params, isolate execution, restrict to file-write only. Which 3 features?
cards.a: argument-hint + context: fork + allowed-tools (restricted to file writes).
cards.why: Each maps to one requirement: param hints on autocomplete, isolated execution, and locked-down tool access.

cards.q: 2-3 exemplar endpoint implementations help generation, but you don't want them loaded during unrelated tasks. How?
cards.a: Create a skill referencing the exemplars + pattern-following instructions, invoked on-demand via slash command.
cards.why: Loads the context only when generating endpoints — not during bug fixes or reviews. Manual copy-paste is tedious and error-prone.

cards.q: An /explore-alternatives skill's brainstorming leaks into later implementation work. Fix?
cards.a: Add context: fork to the skill's frontmatter so it runs isolated.
cards.why: Keeps abandoned approaches out of the main history. Moving it to ~/.claude/skills/ only changes discoverability, not isolation.

cards.q: CLAUDE.md is 500+ lines mixing TS conventions, testing, API rules. How to organize?
cards.a: Split into separate topic files in .claude/rules/ (e.g. testing.md, api-conventions.md).
cards.why: Organizes large instruction sets into focused, maintainable modules.

cards.q: Adding Slack notifications — multiple valid approaches (webhooks/bot tokens/Slack Apps), requirements ambiguous. First move?
cards.a: Enter plan mode to explore the options + trade-offs, then present a recommendation before implementing.
cards.why: Multiple valid architectures + ambiguity = explore and align before committing to a path.

cards.q: You want a personal version of the team's project /commit skill. What's the catch?
cards.a: Project skills take precedence over personal skills of the same name — give yours a different name (e.g. /my-commit).
cards.why: A different name in ~/.claude/skills/ keeps your version accessible alongside the team's.

cards.q: 400+ line CLAUDE.md mixing universal standards with task-specific workflows. How to split?
cards.a: Keep universal standards in CLAUDE.md (always loaded); move task workflows into Skills with trigger keywords.
cards.why: CLAUDE.md loads every conversation (always-on); Skills invoke on-demand when Claude detects relevant keywords.

cards.q: Adding error handling across 120 files: Phase 1 discovery is verbose, Phases 2-3 need retained context. How?
cards.a: Use the Explore subagent for Phase 1 to isolate the verbose output and return a summary; continue Phases 2-3 in the main conversation.
cards.why: Preserves the main context window for the design and implementation phases where retained context matters most.

cards.q: Claude keeps misunderstanding a data transformation's field nesting + timestamp format. Best fix?
cards.a: Provide 2-3 concrete input→output examples showing the expected transformation.
cards.why: Eliminates prose ambiguity by showing exactly what's expected. A JSON schema only verifies structure — it doesn't teach the logic.

cards.q: Agent hits 55% first-contact resolution (target 80%); logs show unclear escalate-vs-resolve boundaries. First fix?
cards.a: Add explicit escalation criteria to the system prompt with few-shot examples of when to escalate vs resolve.
cards.why: Addresses the root cause (unclear decision boundaries) — proportionate, no new infrastructure.

cards.q: Agent averages 4+ API round-trips per resolution (sequential tool calls). Minimal-change fix?
cards.a: Prompt Claude to batch related tool requests in a single turn and return all results together before the next API call.
cards.why: Leverages Claude's native multi-tool ability with minimal change. Composite tools add maintenance burden for every combination.

cards.q: A multi-concern request causes redundant data fetching + many tool calls. Best approach?
cards.a: Decompose into distinct concerns, investigate each in parallel using shared customer context, then synthesize.
cards.why: Reuses context across concerns (no redundant fetches) and parallelizes the investigations.

cards.q: The agent's explanations for complex cases are inconsistently complete. Fix without human review?
cards.a: Add a self-critique step (evaluator-optimizer): evaluate the draft for completeness before sending.
cards.why: Catches case-specific gaps (policy context, timelines, next steps) that vary across scenarios — no human in the loop.

cards.q: Progressive summarization at 70% context loses precise details (amounts, dates, order #s). Fix?
cards.a: Extract transactional facts into a persistent "case facts" block included in every prompt, outside the summarized history.
cards.why: Summarization is lossy for precise details; a structured facts block keeps them reliably available regardless of turn count.

cards.q: Agent calls get_customer for order questions — picks the wrong tool. Low-effort, high-leverage first step?
cards.a: Expand each tool's description: input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries (when to use it vs similar tools).
cards.why: Descriptions are the LLM's primary tool-selection input. A keyword routing layer is over-engineered and brittle.

cards.q: Agent frequently picks get_customer for order lookups. First diagnostic step?
cards.a: Review the tool descriptions to ensure they clearly distinguish each tool's purpose.
cards.why: Descriptions guide selection — check them before building a separate router/classifier that bypasses native selection.

cards.q: Single-concern requests: 94% accuracy. Multi-concern messages fail. Fix?
cards.a: Add few-shot examples demonstrating correct reasoning + tool sequence for multi-concern requests.
cards.why: It already handles single concerns well — it just needs the multi-concern pattern. A separate decompose model-call is over-engineered.

cards.q: When users say "account," correct tool selection drops to 78% (vs 93% without the word). Tool descriptions are clean. Root cause?
cards.a: The system prompt contains keyword-sensitive instructions steering behavior on terms like "account."
cards.why: The systematic keyword-triggered pattern points to prompt-level routing logic, not a training-data gap. Fine-tuning is disproportionate.

cards.q: Customer wants a competitor price match; policy covers own-site drops within 14 days but is silent on competitors. Agent should?
cards.a: Escalate for policy interpretation.
cards.why: A genuine policy gap — the agent can't fabricate policy and needs human judgment to interpret/extend the rules.

cards.q: get_customer returns multiple matches when searching by name (15% wrong-customer rate). Fix?
cards.a: Instruct Claude to ask for an additional identifier (email, phone, order #) when there are multiple matches, before acting.
cards.why: The user has definitive knowledge of their own identity — one extra turn eliminates the wrong-customer error rate.

cards.q: Implementing the agentic loop — how do you know when to continue vs stop after each API call?
cards.a: Check the stop_reason field: continue on "tool_use", stop on "end_turn".
cards.why: It's Claude's explicit, structured loop-control signal: tool_use = run a tool and return results; end_turn = response complete.

cards.q: Agent sometimes picks get_customer when lookup_order is right, on ambiguous requests. How to structure few-shot examples?
cards.a: Add 4-6 examples targeting the ambiguous scenarios, each showing reasoning for why one tool was chosen over a plausible alternative.
cards.why: Teaches comparative decision-making by contrasting tools. Grouping examples by tool teaches each in isolation — not the needed contrast.

cards.topic: testing the shared feature to Deck.json
cards.q: do y'all see this shared deck? xD
cards.a: the way to share is at the bottom of a deck you're currently switched to you should be able to click share to library.
cards.why: the purpose is to have a platform to share topics and would be fun to challenge other areas and maybe later do some analytics with claude and implementing claude to fetch/create questions through this this... I'm aware this feature exists in claude, but thought it would be nice to have a place for studying... wish i did this earlier, but honestly there will be many more cert opportunities for VGV in the future should we want to share studying here too.  Anywho, Good Luck to all! <3

cards.q: testing
cards.why: ugh
cards.a: another answer

decks.name: Claude Interfaces: claude.ai vs Desktop vs CLI

cards.q: Which of the following best describe Claude Code's primary use cases?
cards.why: Claude Code is a CLI tool designed for agentic, code-centric workflows — not browser-based chat or GUI document creation.
choices: Agentic coding tasks run from the terminal
Casual chat and brainstorming in a browser
File creation, editing, and bash command execution
Generating AI-powered slide decks via GUI

cards.q: Which features are available on claude.ai but NOT in Claude Code CLI?
cards.why: Artifacts and Deep Research are claude.ai-specific UI features. Bash execution belongs to Claude Code; MCP is available across contexts.
choices: Bash command execution on local machine

cards.q: The Claude Desktop app is best described as:
cards.why: The Desktop app's key differentiator is native OS integration and MCP server support, enabling local tool connections not possible in the browser.
choices: A wrapper around claude.ai with added local file system access
A standalone native app for macOS/Windows supporting MCP servers and local integrations
A GUI frontend exclusively for the Claude API
An offline version of Claude with no internet requirement

cards.q: A developer wants Claude to autonomously read files, run tests, and commit code. Which interface is appropriate?
cards.why: This scenario requires Claude Code (CLI), not claude.ai. claude.ai is a chat interface; it cannot execute shell commands or directly access the local file system.

cards.q: Which statements about memory are true for claude.ai?
cards.why: Memory generation and user-editable controls are claude.ai features; Incognito disables memory, and memories are per-user, not shared.
choices: Claude can generate and store memories across conversations
Memory is enabled in Incognito conversations by default
Users can edit or delete stored memories
Memory is shared across all users of the same account

cards.q: Which models are currently available via the Anthropic API? (Select all that apply)
cards.why: Claude Mythos Preview is not publicly available — it is restricted to trusted organizations under Project Glasswing and cannot be accessed via the standard API.

cards.q: Claude Code is installed via:
cards.why: Claude Code is a Node.js-based CLI tool distributed through npm, unlike the Desktop app which uses a native installer.

cards.q: Which interface(s) natively support connecting to local MCP servers without additional configuration?
cards.why: Both the Desktop app and Claude Code CLI are designed to connect to local MCP servers. The browser-based claude.ai connects to cloud MCP endpoints, not local ones.

cards.q: A team wants shared conversation history, admin controls, and priority access. Which plan fits?
cards.why: Team and Enterprise plans add admin controls, shared workspaces, and organizational features beyond what Pro (individual) or Free tiers provide.

cards.q: Match each use case to the most appropriate Claude interface: (1) Non-developer automating file/task management, (2) Embedding Claude in a product, (3) Pair programming in VS Code.
cards.why: Interface selection depends on the user's technical level and integration depth — Cowork targets non-developers, the API is for builders, and Claude Code extensions integrate directly into IDEs.
cards.a: (1) Cowork (desktop automation tool), (2) Anthropic API, (3) Claude Code for VS Code extension
